one_line_takeaway: LLM-PBE is a unified toolkit and empirical study showing that larger/more-capable LLMs leak memorized training data, system prompts, and inferred user attributes more readily, with DP fine-tuning the only defense that meaningfully cuts risk while prompt-based defenses fail.
key_finding: Privacy risk scales with model capability across every attack channel: for Pythia the data-extraction accuracy on Enron rises with model size faster than the ARC-Easy utility gain, and the synthetic-email control extracts ~0%, confirming genuine memorization rather than reasoning. Data characteristics matter: contextual PII (locations) and data at the END of a sample are extracted most (Llama-2 7B-FT location DEA 19.2% overall vs name 10.4%, date 6.7%), and higher-perplexity (harder-to-predict) samples yield higher MIA AUC (ECHR >200 tokens AUC 82.2%). Larger models within a family leak system prompts more (Llama-2-70b LR@90FR 83.0% vs 7b 56.7%) and are more susceptible to attribute inference (Claude AIA rises 35.4% -> 87.1% tracking MMLU 63.4% -> 88.7%), while jailbreak success DROPS with size (Llama-2 MoP 72.4% at 7B -> 58.9% at 70B). Defenses: DP-SGD (epsilon=8) is the strongest PET, cutting Refer MIA AUC from 97.7% to 49.0% and DEA from 24.2% to 3.2% on ECHR; scrubbing helps (DEA 4.0%) but badly degrades utility; manually crafted defensive prompts are essentially useless (GPT-4 LR@90FR 80.7% -> best 79.3%).
core_contribution: The paper's OWN contribution is LLM-PBE (LLM Privacy BEnchmark), an open-source Python toolkit and accompanying empirical study for systematically assessing data-privacy leakage across the full LLM lifecycle (pretraining data, fine-tuning data, and system/instruction prompts). Unlike prior work that each covered a single data type, a few models, or one attack, LLM-PBE unifies four attack families (data extraction, membership inference, prompt leaking, jailbreak) with four defenses (scrubbing, differential privacy, machine unlearning, defensive prompting) over multiple data types (PII, copyrighted code, domain knowledge, prompts) and both black-box (GPT-3.5/4) and white-box (Llama-2, Pythia) models. The authors use it to run their own experiments isolating the effects of model size, data characteristics, and model release time on privacy risk. (Note: Table 1's DecodingTrust/CONFAIDE/LiRA/Jailbroken/etc. are cited prior benchmarks used only to position coverage, not this paper's results.)
threat_model: Primary threat model (Section 3.5) is an adversary with black-box query access to a deployed LLM (query in, text out; no weights, no gradients, no training-data access). Two leakage channels are studied: (1) leakage of memorized training/fine-tuning corpus, and (2) breach of hidden system/instruction prompts imprinted during customization. Query-based data extraction, comparison-based membership inference, prompt-leaking, and jailbreak attacks all operate under this black-box assumption; the poisoning-based DEA variant additionally assumes the attacker can inject data into the fine-tuning set, which the authors flag as a stronger, less realistic assumption. A separate Section 6 attribute-inference threat assumes the adversary supplies user-written context to a model to infer private user attributes.
limitations: The authors explicitly caveat that all findings are contingent on the specific existing attack/defense methods integrated and may not hold for future methods. Coverage is broad but each configuration is often a single representative run; MIAs can only be evaluated on fine-tuning data (membership labels unknown for pretraining). The PET study measures only privacy reduction and explicitly ignores overfitting/utility trade-off quantification beyond noting scrubbing degrades performance. The poisoning DEA underperformed, which the authors attribute to possibly suboptimal poison-pattern design rather than a robustness conclusion. Enron/ECHR/GitHub memorization is inferred from public-training-set assumptions rather than certified ground truth. Attribute-inference correctness is judged by GPT-4, introducing judge bias. Scope is text LLMs only (vision/multimodal left to future work).

datasets_used: Enron email corpus (~500,000 emails; PII / email-address extraction)
ECHR - European Court of Human Rights (11.5k cases; domain-knowledge / fine-tuning data)
GitHub Python code (22,133 repos with >500 stars, 10.5GB; copyrighted-work extraction)
SynthPAI (7,823 synthetic comments, 4,730 attributes; attribute inference)
Synthetic email dataset (Enron-format control to test memorization vs reasoning)
quantitative_results: Pythia DEA on Enron increases with model size (near-0 at 70m up to ~6% at 12b) and outpaces ARC-Easy utility gains; synthetic-email DEA ~0% (memorization confirmed)
ECHR DEA by PII type on Llama-2 7B-FT (fine-tuned 4 epochs): location 19.2% overall, name 10.4%, date 6.7% (vs plain Llama-2 7B: location 2.6%, name 0.81%, date 0.30%)
DEA higher for private data at END of sample (e.g., Llama-2 7B-FT location End 24.4% vs Front 7.7%)
Refer-MIA AUC rises with sample length: ECHR (0,50] 55.9% -> (200,inf] 82.2%
PET on fine-tuned ECHR - Refer MIA AUC: none 97.7%, scrubbing 87.3%, DP(eps=8) 49.0%; DEA: none 24.2%, scrubbing 4.0%, DP 3.2%; PPL-MIA none 97.9% -> DP 50.9%
Jailbreak success DECREASES with size - Llama-2 model-generated (MoP): 7B 72.4%, 13B 68.0%, 70B 58.9%; manual (MaP) 58.2/56.7/47.4%
Prompt leakage LR@90FR: llama-2-70b 83.0%, gpt-4 80.7%, vicuna-13b 74.0%, gpt-3.5 67.0%, llama-2-7b 56.7%; vicuna-13b LR@99FR 64.0%
Defensive prompting on GPT-4 negligible: no-defense LR@90FR 80.7% -> best defense 79.3%
Claude attribute-inference (AIA) accuracy tracks MMLU: C-2.1 35.4% (MMLU 63.4%) -> C-3.5-sonnet 87.1% (MMLU 88.7%)
Efficiency (Llama-2 7B, Enron, 2x H100): plain inference 28GB/0.43s, plain training 112GB/0.59s; inference-based attacks ~1x cost, model-based MIA infeasible for LLMs
baselines_compared: MIA methods benchmarked against each other: PPL (perplexity threshold), Refer (reference-model log-perplexity ratio), LiRA (likelihood ratio), MIN-K (min-probability tokens)
DEA variants: query-based (prefix prompting) vs poisoning-based
Jailbreak: manually designed 15 templates (MaP) vs model-generated prompts (MoP)
PLA attack prompts: what-was, translation, repeat_w_head, encode_base64, spell_check, ignore_print (6-8 variants)
Coverage comparison (Table 1, prior benchmarks, NOT this paper's runs): DecodingTrust, GPLM, CONFAIDE, LiRA, Neighbor, MI-LLM, Jailbroken, PromptExtraction, PromptInject
Defenses compared: no-defense vs scrubbing (Flair) vs DP-SGD (FastDP); five defensive prompts (no-repeat, top-secret, ignore-ignore-inst, no-ignore, eaten)
